text:
This paper applies a global and transnational perspective on the history of religion and aims to shed new light on the dynamic processes of early modern globalization, centred on the globally acting Jesuit Order with its enormous missionary network during the second half of the 17th and in the 18th centuries. Focusing on the Pimería Alta – i.e. modern day Sonora and Arizona – the article explains the unusual large contingent of German Jesuit missionaries that under the auspices of the earlier mentioned pioneer-missionary Eusebio Kino streaming into this huge colonial mission area. In this context, the interdisciplinary working Jesuits as missionaries, at the same time representing scientists, geographers and ethnographers, that stretched the usually colonial isolated missions (or stations, or settlements) from the remote colonial periphery towards the metropolis of Europe.

With regard to the title of the paper, it can be explained by the fact that the “Welt-Bott” is a monumental collection of Jesuit correspondence, compiled by Joseph Stöcklein. From these letters and reports the author reconstructs the world-wide communication system which the Jesuits had created to transmit scientific knowledge, geographical information and cultural achievements. Although the Order was suppressed in the late eighteenth century and thus its historical memory was split up into numerous pieces, the Jesuit correspondence allows us – unexpectedly – to record in an unique way an impressive number of transcultural contacts and to document in great detail the first scientific attempts to explore the New World.
